speaker: interviewer
text: The family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is Eve, my wife. We've been married more than 30 years now. EVE (WIFE): 41. [LAUGHS] Oh, 40-- 41. [? You ?] [? had ?] me worried there. [LAUGHS] I lost-- I lost the-- I lost the count. Of course, a year more than 40. That's Daniel, my son, his wife, Michelle, and the apple of my eye, Lisa, our only granddaughter. Lisa is 17. Lovely age to be. EVE (WIFE): Come on, enough of this [? conversation. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Daniel, when did you find out that your father was a Holocaust survivor? DANIEL (SON): I don't know. It's always been that-- that both my parents survived the-- the war. When did your father start talking to you about it? DANIEL (SON): He only really started getting very talkative on the subject since he retired. And were you able to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: DANIEL (SON): I think we've had very little choice most of the time, to listen. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice when you were growing up that your life was in any way different from that of your friends? DANIEL (SON): I don't think so. Because of survivorship?

speaker: interviewee
text: DANIEL (SON): I don't think so. Occasionally there was a feeling in the house of some big, bad secret from years gone by. But they-- they came and went very infrequently. And really, there's nothing that I could see that was much different to what  I was doing, compared to my friends. What about your grandparents? You know, as a-- as a boy of 5, you made visits to Haifa, to your grandparents, who are also survivors. DANIEL (SON): Mm. I mean, they were in hiding with Eve. EVE (WIFE): Well, that-- that's how they knew. Did you notice-- did you notice anything special there? DANIEL (SON): It's like going to Germany, that's all. [LAUGHS] Because they're all-- they're all the German manner and-- It's [INAUDIBLE] too, you know? DANIEL (SON): Yeah. So that's about it, really. No, I-- I don't think there was anything untoward from it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lisa, is there anything that you would like to say your grandfather? LISA (GRANDDAUGHTER): No.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No. Well, you can ask me. I don't know what to say. I-- I don't really have anything to say, spur of the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask you the same questions I asked Daniel. Is-- when did you find out about your grandparents being survivors? LISA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I've always known. So it's like, I can't remember a time when he didn't talk about it. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --I've known all along. But it never seemed like-- it never seemed like some terrible thing. Like, or a secret or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody else like to contribute? EVE (WIFE): I don't want to contribute because I'll be very negative about it. So I don't want to spoil your fun. So I won't contribute to anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I just find it interesting that Michael wants to talk about it all the time. And Eve doesn't want to talk about it. So it's-- it's quite-- as somebody's coming into the family, that was an interesting thing to see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to listen when Michael was-- wanted to talk? MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I think I listened, don't I?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Until you're told to change the subject anyway, I listen. DANIEL (SON): You listen the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Do I? Who says-- who says that, to change the subject? MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Eve, usually.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] EVE (WIFE): Well, I think it's-- MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): But we all join in. [LAUGHS] EVE (WIFE): I think it's become fashionable to talk about it now. So everybody is sort of into it. But it just brings back the memories everyone wants to forget. And it hasn't done Michael any good. I've been-- as we said, we've been married for 41 years. And he was much happier, and a jollier person before all this started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can't see any benefit that has happened to him since he got so busy with it. And all these interviews just regurgitate the same old story. You can't change the past. You might as well look into the future. So I shall never agree with these things. But I'm always, somehow, roped into it, even tonight. I was going to be out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] But I thought, I'll collect him. And I was-- got roped into this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for coming this evening and talking to us. And thank you, all of you. EVE (WIFE): Thank you. LISA (GRANDDAUGHTER): OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're looking at a photograph of my mother with her youngest brother, around 1917, when my uncle was still in the Austrian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle is called Isaac Engelberg, Uncle Itzchak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is he in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the soldier at the back of the photograph, now in closeup. You can see that he's got three stars. I don't know what his rank was in the army. EVE (WIFE): [INAUDIBLE]. That's my mother, in her youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [? Celia, ?] or [? Cecily ?] Honigwachs, born Engelberg. That's my eldest brother, Leo. I, of course, didn't know him like this. Because I wasn't born in 1917. I was born in 1929. That's my brother, Henry. Henry was 16 years older than me. And Leo, whom we saw just a while ago, was 18 years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken somewhere in Novy Jicin, when my uncle came to visit. My mother moved to Novy Jicin from Vienna, in the first World War, because my father was stationed in Novy Jicin in the barracks there. He was working in the stalls in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is a bit later than that, possibly around 1928, perhaps '26. The girl behind is my cousin, Gutja, who died in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutja Besen. Dr. Besen was a lawyer in Krakow, married the youngest sister of my father. And Gutja was, as you see, a lovely-looking young girl, very clever. We don't even know where she perished. The boy that she's holding is Leo, my eldest brother. The next boy on the left is Henry. And my third brother is [? Shraga, ?] the youngest then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was born in 1920. So he was about five there, I suppose. That was also taken in Novy Jicin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't make out who the person is on the left there of Gutja, when the photograph was taken, because someone had cut this photograph in half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man on my-- on the right of the photograph is my father. His name is Shlomo Honigwachs, or Salomon Honigwachs. This is taken in Split, on the Adriatic, when my father was there in 1935, on holiday, when my mother actually went to visit her sister in Palestine. I don't know the two gentlemen in the photograph. Pop-- I presume they are some people that he met at the seaside. Can you manage to get the girl behind as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, can you see the girl is really wearing a yellow star? And this was taken in Valasske Mezrici, in Moravia. This is a brother and sister of a family called-- [? Stein, ?] something [? Stein. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did I say? Not [? Levinstein. ?] They were the only Jewish family in the town who had a farm. They were farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as you can see, they were sort of-- --basic farmers. You now, it was all horse-drawn stuff and cows, and-- and my brother, [? Milos, ?] worked with them. And I suppose he took the picture. I don't know who took the picture. But it's about 1940, this picture. Both of these people perished in the Shoah. Because nobody came back from Valasske Mezrici, not a single one. EVE (WIFE): That's [INAUDIBLE]. This is my brother, Leo, with his wife, Traute. I chose this picture mainly because of the stars. It's not a good picture of either of them. But this is about 1940, when they got married. And they lived in Prague. My brother went to Theresienstadt in 1941. Traute followed him about six months later. My brother, Leo, died in the Holocaust, perhaps a week before the war was over. Somewhere with a hospital, which was evacuated by the Germans. And neither the patients, nor the doctors survived. Traute survived in Bergen-Belsen. We brought her to this country. She could only get a permit to come as a domestic servant. But poor woman, she died of cancer in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my brother, Emmanuel, or [? Milos, ?] as we called him. He was a-- not-- today, this morning, when I found this picture, I said to myself, he was a lovely-looking boy. And who-- who would kill children like this? I mean, he was 16 when he went into the camps. When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken about 1941, perhaps '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Valasske Mezrici. And he would have been about 14 at the time. In the 1990s, after the Velvet Revolution, we started going back to my hometown in Novy Jicin, in Czechoslovakia. I'm talking about me and my brother, [? Shraga, ?] from Israel. My brother, [? Shraga, ?] discovered this in the archives. And I don't know if you can make out-- it's-- it's a book called from Dr. Paul Ziegler. The remarkable thing, if you can hone in at the bottom of the page, is the date when it was published.

speaker: interviewer
text: That, incidentally, is the sign of Neutitschein, that-- that emblem. But can you see it was published in 1939? It's a journal of the 68th issue published in 1939. Now, the remarkable thing about this is that Dr. Paul Ziegler was the mayor of the city, the German mayor of the city, in 1930-- in the early 1930s.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- when Hitler came to power, the new elections brought the Nazi Party. It was called the Henlein Party, in Czechoslovakia. Into-- and-- and there was a-- a new-- a new mayor. And Dr. Ziegler retired. He wrote a book about the Jews of Novy Jicin, this book. But in 1939, although he was not a Nazi, and he was not in the Nazi Party, nevertheless, in 1939, he added a few bits. When you read the book, you can see the edit bits. And it made it sort of a Nazi book. And the tenor of the book is describing the history of the Jews of Novy Jicin. Because the Jews were expelled from the town in 1456. And they drifted back in various times. And he says, in the book, we've been trying to get rid of them ever since. And in 1939, they had done. Because all the Czech Jews that lived there were expelled. And now, Neutitschein is clear of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? INT: ?] (WHISPERS) [? Finished. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a postcard which my mother wrote to my brother, Shraga, just before he went on Aliyah to Palestine. He is staying somewhere at a Czech address in Bohemia, before they all left on this illegal transport. And my brother, Shraga, was in charge of the group. It was the last group to leave Czechoslovakia to go to Palestine. My mother merely says that she sent the documents where he asked they should be sent, nothing more. We-- this is a typical card of the central card index, which was kept in Theresienstadt, and which was saved by the Jewish community after Theresienstadt was freed, and is now available for people to look up every person who went through Theresienstadt. And this happens to be my card, which shows that I arrived in Theresienstadt on the 18th of September, 1942. And I left for Auschwitz on the 15th of December, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The man on the left is my brother, Leo, in Prague, with some friends. I don't know any of them. And I don't know how I have the photograph. It was somewhere in my father's possession. And it's sort of 1939-ish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a rather interesting photograph at the pump outside our flat where we stayed, in the house of the man who stands on the right. That's Mr. Lypszyc, was. Mr. Lypszyc saved himself a lot of trouble at the beginning of 1939, because he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is the one second from the left, standing with her hands folded. The lady who is holding the pump handle is Mrs. Berman, from Novy Jicin. These are the people that I stayed one night with when I came back to Novy Jicin in 1945. The girl behind is Mrs. Berman's daughter, Kathe. They came to visit us from Novy Jicin. And the other lady is a Mrs. Langer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the lady in spotted dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the spotted dress, yes. Here's Mrs. Langer, who I hear-- somehow from the family, I seem to remember that the Langers went to Australia. I lost, completely, track of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The two boys, the one on the right is [? Milos, ?] my brother. And the one on the left is me, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my brother, Henry, sometime after 1945, when he was somewhere at the seaside, playing tennis. Henry sort of was a good sportsman. He-- he was good at everything he did. But he was a jolly sort of bloke. He was here through-- throughout the war. When Henry was dying a year and  a half ago, the thing that gripped him in  the last dying days was this story of how he escaped across the border to Poland, and caught a ship to join my father in London; when he arranged with my brother, Leo, that he would ship us two boys secretly, you know, by a train, by a goods train to Poland. And my brother, Leo, then wouldn't do it. Or he talked with my mother. And my mother wouldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you mentioned this on the video.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned it on the video. That was his-- his problem throughout life was why did my mother not move? This is a memorial stone which was put up at the Jewish Cemetery in Novy Jicin, in 1948, when the Jewish Cemetery was still standing. The cemetery was devastated several times by the Czechs during the Communist times, and now has been completely cleared. And the mortuary of the cemetery was added onto. You know, an extension was built. And it's now an Adventist Church. We've honed down onto the name of my mother and my brother, Leo, and my brother, Emmanuel, who are listed here as part of about 98 people who died as a result of the Holocaust. The other little interesting thing here is that my mother's name, Honigwachsova, the S is missing. So there is a spelling mistake on the stone. It's still there after 50 years. [LAUGHS] This is a nice family picture. This must be about 1948 or so, because it shows my brother, Shraga, his wife, [? Chanka, ?] their son on the left, Oded, their eldest son, Ronit, his-- his sister, and Nadav, the small child. That's-- they have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, when-- when was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1948. It's taken in Kfar Ruppin.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. This is my wife, Eve, and I. It's unbelievable that it's us, because we look so young on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1956. EVE (WIFE): '54. What, before we were married? EVE (WIFE): Yeah. OK. The boss spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken? Which country? EVE (WIFE): In Cambridge, [? the ?] [? summer. ?] Cambridge,  I think. In England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Cambridge, yes. The visit to Cambridge, possibly. This is my brother, Mordche. This is also taken in Israel, in Kfar Ruppin, where they all lived. Kibbutz, Kfar  Ruppin, in [INAUDIBLE] [? Beit ?] [? She'an. ?] His wife, on the left, is Ruta Hon. Mordche called himself Hon. And their children, Rina, the girl standing behind them, and her-- her younger brother, [? Giora. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture when my brother, Shraga, visited us last in England. And I think it's taken on Hampstead Heath, with my brother, Henry, on the right. He was then the eldest. He was the second-eldest surviving brother. And Shraga is on my left. And it's about 1985. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is in Novy Jicin, in 1992. My brother, Shraga, arranged a visit by all the survivors of Novy Jicin, all the generations as well. You can see some young people behind. The man on my right, behind me, is Max Mannheimer. He has since died. He was the foremost dealer in antique watches in the world. He lived in Switzerland, became extremely rich. EVE (WIFE): [INAUDIBLE]. But he was my brother's pal. Behind him stands [? Yendareich. ?] To the right, that young man, is a man called [? Seba. ?] He lives here in England. I'm entering the gymnasium where I dedicated this plaque, which was corrected in the school where my brother, Shraga, came to visit. And he saw this dedication to the people who perished in the Holocaust. And he said to them, look, you've got all these people missing. And they started searching the archives. And they added my brother, Emmanuel, and the brother of the Mannheimer's called [? Eric, ?] who was also in this school, and all these people on the right. So we-- we sort of had a little ceremony around this during the memorial days. We had three memorial days. I sort of think of it as a poignant time. Because my brother arranged it all. And then during the time when it took place, he was in hospital, dying because he-- [? MICHELLE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Because he-- he had a heart bypass operation, which didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in 1994, in a place called-- in a place called-- oh, on the Sazava River, at a hotel. We had a meeting of all the people from the group [INAUDIBLE] in Theresien, and all the people who were the Birkenau boys. And one or two of the Madrichim came. Now, we're looking at the left-hand side of the picture. That's Harry Goldberger. He's a Birkenau boy. But he was in room seven in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just name--

speaker: interviewee
text: The next man is Yehuda Backon, blue shirt. Yehuda Backon is a painter, lives in Jerusalem. He's quite a famous painter. The next man is one of the Madrichim, [? Fisher, ?] Avi [? Fisher. ?] Avi [? Fisher ?] became a teacher in Israel. He died a couple of years ago. The man sticking his head out behind Avi is me. That next man, you can rest on him for a bit. That's [? Kyle ?] [? Chabon. ?] That's a really Czech name. He now uses the name [? Kleint. ?] That's the gentlemen with his arms folded? Arms folded, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is just behind him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just behind him is [? Skoba. ?] Now, I forget his surname. But he lives in Mexico, Mexico City.

speaker: interviewer
text: And standing in front of him with his arms, holding his hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Pavel Kohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lives in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him is Paul Laupert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Open shirt. He was director of the Gymnasium School of the Kibbutzim [INAUDIBLE] and others, near Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the gentleman with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the man with the folded arms is Karl Langer. Lives in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him is Tommy Frankel. He's a film-- he's in the film business, cameramen, and makes films.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the gentlemen with the white shorts.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's [? Hakiki. ?] That's Hunzer Polak, who escaped with me from Gunskirchen. And the man with-- with his hands in his pocket is [? Schmudla ?] [? Klauber. ?] He was in [INAUDIBLE] room number five [INAUDIBLE]. I never got on with [? Schmudla. ?] And we still don't get on really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him is Harry [? Zilbersten. ?] He lives in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's one of the Birkenau boys. And the last on the right, white hat, is Otto Jakubowicz. He lives in Sheffield. And he's from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding in 19-- '90-- no, when did they get married? --'94, I suppose. In the middle is Daniel, next to his bride, Michelle. You've seen them before on the-- on the tape. The girl in front is Lisa. She was a bridesmaid. Michelle is not Lisa's mother. This is Daniel's second marriage. I'm sitting-- I'm standing behind Lisa. On the right of Michelle is my wife, Eve. And on her right is my youngest son, Jonathan. Jonathan David Honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Liverpool, as a wedding photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: In England?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got a whole video, which we haven't got time to show. [LAUGHS] But it's taken in England, yes.
